item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was with this until the end, but unfortunately I can’t upvote it because I feel that the ending defeats the concept of the piece.

It’s a murder mystery, detective fiction, and the most important, non-negotiable element of good detective fiction is that the mystery can be figured out using the clues provided to the audience.

But the addition of ‘SCP-5037-0’, the teenager’s suicide, was an element brought it at the last minute, without which the mystery could not be solved. It felt unsatisfying, like if it turned out that the butler did it without a butler ever having been mentioned before.

So I’m no-voting. I liked the concept, I was hooked throughout, and I’ll happily upvote if you find some way to integrate that bit of information into the initial setup of the mystery.

First of all, thank you so much for the more than 50 upvotes! Considering this is the literally unfinished and generally kind of half assed version, that y'all liked it so much regardless means a lot!

I'll be doing significant additions to get it to the intended final version once it's settled in its new slot, and I'll also be doing significant revisions of the current material based on all of your feedback! The investigation logs are going to be basically completely rewritten, so look forward to it!

A Random Day, TacoTheDank, Demento56, TL333s, and Zesc, thank you so much for the praise! It means a ton and motivates me to make it even better!

gee0765 and Uncannyon, I'll be revamping and revitalizing Isaac's role so that Level 0 stuff really matters, and that'll prove especially true with the material I didn't have time to add. Thank you so much for the critique thereof; it's something I'll definitely be fixing!

psul, HammerMaiden, Modern_Erasmus, the relative ease with which things were solved and the corny dialogue without a strong sense of distinctiveness or character voice are both major points I plan to fix as completely as possible in revamps, revisions, and added material! I'll definitely address those concerns!

Once again, thank you so much for all the feedback, positive and critical alike!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, the dialog nearly lost me, it's so damn corny and melodramatic. It reads like a pulp detective novel and isn't normally in my style of things that I like.

…But it's on point and the style serves the narrative in ways more formal OR more naturally conversational dialog wouldn't.

+1. Begrudgingly, but +1. It justifies itself well enough to live.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The hook here (in the form of the first note's text) is one of the strongest I've seen in a long time, and I really like the ending too (both the solution of the mystery, and the final addendum). But this suffered in the middle from ultra-self-conscious dialogue (to the extent that the two characters' voices were difficult to distinguish), which destroyed my suspension of disbelief. The solution was also relatively unfair to readers (or perhaps being arrived at too quickly) without a full set of facts to draw on. I think that with some major edits (like essentially doubling the length of the investigation text, and ditching a lot of the current transcripts in favour of more obvious in-universe documents), this could be a truly excellent skip, but in its current form it doesn't capitalise on its best elements.

item.title: Why did i just read this now?
item.content:encoded: I mean… you literally took the contest's theme and made it an SCP. This is awsome. If asked right now, this is my favourite 5000. Ask again later, prolly still top three.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really appreciate the detective fiction here, and it comes to a very satisfying conclusion. This just tells a great story, full stop.

That said, I detest the introduction of your detective. He's such a smarmy jackass, I want to wipe the smug grin off his damned face. Which, realizing that his expression is never described, means he's an effective character, but ugh D:

I have to admit to having a few mixed feelings about this piece.

Personally, the Level 0 thing didn't seem to make a ton of sense to me. It's not to say I dislike the idea of a low security rating being used that way, but it didn't seem to have much of a role within this particular story. None of their mystery-solving work was really improved by having an outsider's perspective, and it just felt somewhat redundant.

That being said, I enjoyed the plot. I think it was a rather fun mystery, and a pretty interesting reason for an SCP to be perpetuating itself.

I could have done with a little more on how the effect began, or a few more clues pointing towards the solution, but overall I certainly think I enjoyed it enough to give it a solid +1

Nice work

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I went in to this not thinking it was a good fit for the 5000 contest, and, as I type this, I have to say I come out of it thinking the same way.

Don't get me wrong, I love the concept and it's a 10/10 for storytelling, it's just that other 1000 slot scips have been much bigger things - 1000 was a whole other dominant race, 2000 was the Foundation restarting civilization at will, 3000 was an eldritch source of all amnestics, and 4000 was a much bigger concept than this one.

It's a no-vote from me for now, but it'll be a +1 after the contest, no doubt.

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
[+ Opinion]
– hide block

This was pretty good; however, I'm not sure I quite get the logic of keeping the investigator at Level 0. The justification for keeping them at the clearance level didn't really work for me, as they're in a sort of middle ground between knowing and not knowing which doesn't quite slide with me. Due to their knowledge that anomalies do in fact exist, the advantages given in the log don't apply that much. Anyone can imagine a monster that walks through walls, and they even have confirmation that that sort of thing exists.

it's an anomalous entity that can phase through walls or teleport or some such

Like, this sort of thing is a fairly standard monster in urban myths and such; knowing about the anomalous would lead you to conclusions like this I believe. Giving details about the belief by people who are fully aware of the anomalous also can't help the investigators judgement here, as it goes against why they're useful.

Besides that, though, this kept my interest all the way through, and the ending was intriguing. The blackboxes around the years in dates are entirely unnecessary though, and could just be filled with numbers. I can easily look past my confusion about the clearance and upvote, since I admit this may just be down to personal thoughts more than anything else.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to gee0765 or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: Pretty good
item.content:encoded: Although I doubt this one will win the contest, I really like this one, I love the way it's written. Kudos to you for a good tale :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one. The reveal was sufficiently weird to hook me, and the denouement and epilogue are creepy and leave me wondering what will happen next.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll do a major revamp once the contest is completely done.

You don't have to wait. Edits are allowed to be implemented at any time — there isn't a restriction on this (anymore).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey hi hello, I deadass thought I had an entire extra day further and thus was forced to post a moderately truncated version. Also the picture is one I took myself.

Edit: I'll be finishing it up within the next few days !!!

My preferred slots are 5037, 5073, 5307, 5370, and 5703.
